section.title: The Lost Sheep, Coin, and Son
section.sectionRationale: When the Pharisees grumble that Jesus welcomes sinners and eats with them, He answers with three parables of things lost and found: a shepherd who leaves ninety-nine to seek one sheep, a woman who sweeps her house for one coin, and a father who runs to embrace a son who was dead and is alive again. Each ends in a celebration, and the last leaves an elder brother standing outside, unwilling to rejoice. This is the heart of the gospel: the joy of heaven over one sinner who repents.
section.thesis: Because heaven rejoices over one sinner who repents, God Himself seeks the lost until He finds them and runs to welcome the returning, so the only true response is to come home and to join the Father's joy, not to stand outside it like the elder son.
section.frameNote: Three parables in one movement: the lost sheep sought, the lost coin swept for, and the lost son welcomed home, closing with the father's plea to the elder brother.

units.label: The grumble and the lost sheep
units.note: joy in heaven over one who repents

verses.text: Now all the tax collectors and sinners were gathering around to listen to Jesus.
So the Pharisees and scribes began to grumble: “This man welcomes sinners and eats with them.”
Then Jesus told them this parable:
“What man among you, if he has a hundred sheep and loses one of them, does not leave the ninety-nine in the pasture and go after the one that is lost, until he finds it?
And when he finds it, he joyfully puts it on his shoulders,
comes home, and calls together his friends and neighbors to tell them, ‘Rejoice with me, for I have found my lost sheep!’
I tell you that in the same way there will be more {{chara|joy}} in heaven over one sinner who {{metanoeo|repents}} than over ninety-nine righteous ones who do not need to repent.

units.label: The lost coin
units.note: she searches carefully until she finds it

verses.text: Or what woman who has ten silver coins and loses one of them does not light a lamp, sweep her house, and search carefully until she finds it?
And when she finds it, she calls together her friends and neighbors to say, ‘Rejoice with me, for I have found my lost coin.’
In the same way, I tell you, there is joy in the presence of God’s angels over one sinner who repents.”

units.label: The lost son and the father's welcome
units.note: this son of mine was dead and is alive again

verses.text: Then Jesus said, “There was a man who had two sons.
The younger son said to him, ‘Father, give me my share of the estate.’ So he divided his property between them.
After a few days, the younger son got everything together and journeyed to a distant country, where he squandered his wealth in wild living.
After he had spent all he had, a severe famine swept through that country, and he began to be in need.
So he went and hired himself out to a citizen of that country, who sent him into his fields to feed the pigs.
He longed to fill his belly with the pods the pigs were eating, but no one would give him a thing.
Finally he came to his senses and said, ‘How many of my father’s hired servants have plenty of food, but here I am, starving to death!
I will get up and go back to my father and say to him, “Father, I have sinned against heaven and against you.
I am no longer worthy to be called your son. Make me like one of your hired servants.”’
So he got up and went to his father. But while he was still in the distance, his father saw him and was filled with {{splanchnizomai|compassion}}. He ran to his son, embraced him, and kissed him.
The son declared, ‘Father, I have sinned against heaven and against you. I am no longer worthy to be called your son.’
But the father said to his servants, ‘Quick! Bring the best robe and put it on him. Put a ring on his finger and sandals on his feet.
Bring the fattened calf and kill it. Let us feast and celebrate.
For this son of mine was dead and is alive again! He was {{apollymi|lost}} and is found!’ So they began to celebrate.
Meanwhile the older son was in the field, and as he approached the house, he heard music and dancing.
So he called one of the servants and asked what was going on.
‘Your brother has returned,’ he said, ‘and your father has killed the fattened calf, because he has him back safe and sound.’
The older son became angry and refused to go in. So his father came out and pleaded with him.
But he answered his father, ‘Look, all these years I have served you and never disobeyed a commandment of yours. Yet you never gave me even a young goat so I could celebrate with my friends.
But when this son of yours returns from squandering your wealth with prostitutes, you kill the fattened calf for him!’
‘Son, you are always with me,’ the father said, ‘and all that is mine is yours.
But it was fitting to celebrate and be glad, because this brother of yours was dead and is alive again; he was lost and is found.’”

chara.gloss: Joy, gladness. Each parable ends in joy: the shepherd rejoicing, the woman calling neighbors to rejoice, the father throwing a feast. Jesus lifts this joy to heaven itself, where God and His angels rejoice over one sinner who turns home. The grumbling of the Pharisees is answered by the rejoicing of God.

metanoeo.gloss: To repent, to change one's mind and turn around. The sheep is carried, the coin is swept up, and the son comes to his senses and returns; each pictures repentance as being found by God and coming home to Him. Heaven's joy is over one such sinner who repents.

splanchnizomai.gloss: To be moved with compassion from deep within, in the gut. While the son is still far off, the father is seized with this visceral compassion and runs, an undignified sprint of love. The word reveals the heart of God toward returning sinners: not cold permission but overflowing mercy.

apollymi.gloss: To lose, to be lost, to perish. The same word binds the three parables together: the lost sheep, the lost coin, the lost son. To be lost is to be perishing away from God; to be found is to be restored to life, was dead and is alive again.

basic.context: The tax collectors and sinners crowd around Jesus, and the religious leaders grumble that He welcomes such people and even eats with them. Jesus answers with three parables. A shepherd leaves ninety-nine sheep to find one that is lost and carries it home rejoicing. A woman lights a lamp and sweeps her whole house until she finds one lost coin, then calls her neighbors to celebrate. A father divides his estate, watches his younger son waste it all in a far country, and then, while the boy is still far off, runs to embrace him and throws a feast, for this son was dead and is alive again. But the elder son, dutiful and cold, refuses to come in, and the father pleads with him too.
basic.hermeneutics: Read the three as one answer to the grumble of verse 2. Heaven's joy over the found sinner is the thread. The first two parables stress God's active seeking; the third opens up the heart of the Father and turns the mirror on the grumblers themselves, pictured in the elder brother who will not share his father's joy.
basic.originalLanguages: The word chara, joy, crowns each parable and rises to heaven itself. To be found is to metanoeo, to repent, to come home. The father is moved with splanchnizomai, gut-deep compassion, and runs. And the refrain that binds all three is apollymi, lost: the lost sheep, the lost coin, the lost son, each restored to life.
basic.typology: The seeking shepherd fulfills God's promise in Ezekiel to search for His scattered sheep Himself. The father who runs and clothes his son foreshadows the God who covers the shame of returning sinners. The feast over the found son points to the joy of the kingdom's table and the marriage supper of the Lamb.
basic.christ: Jesus is the shepherd who goes after the one lost sheep and lays it on His shoulders, the seeking love of God in person. He is the one who welcomes sinners and eats with them, embodying the father who runs down the road. In Him God is not waiting stiffly for sinners to grovel but sprinting to meet them.
basic.god: God is revealed as a seeking, running, celebrating Father, moved with compassion for the lost and full of joy when they return. He does not merely permit the sinner back; He watches the road, runs, embraces, clothes, and feasts. And He goes out to plead even with the resentful, self-righteous elder son.
basic.crossReferences: Ezekiel 34 promises that God Himself will seek His lost sheep. Luke 19 shows the Son of Man come to seek and to save the lost. Romans 5 says that while we were still sinners, Christ died for us. 1 Timothy 1 calls Paul the foremost of sinners whom Christ came to save.
basic.oneStory: Against the grumbling of the religious, Jesus unveils the joy at the center of the universe: God seeking the lost until He finds them and running to welcome them home. The story runs from a shepherd's search to a father's embrace, and it presses the question left hanging over the elder brother, will we come in and share the Father's joy?

context.literary: Luke 15 is a single unit of three parables sharing one theme, loss and joyful recovery, told in answer to the objection of verse 2. It is the heart of Luke's travel narrative and often called the gospel within the Gospel. The three build in intensity, from a lost animal to a lost object to a lost child, and the last is left deliberately open.
context.historical: Shepherding and coin-keeping were humble, everyday realities; ten drachmas may have been a woman's dowry or savings. For a younger son to demand his inheritance while the father lived was to wish him dead, and to feed pigs and crave their pods was utter degradation for a Jew. The father running would have shamed himself publicly to reach his son first.
context.canonical: The image of God as shepherd seeking lost sheep runs from Psalm 23 and Ezekiel 34 to John 10 and 1 Peter 2. The joy of heaven over repentance and the feast of restoration anticipate Revelation's wedding supper. The elder brother echoes every warning against a righteousness that despises grace shown to others.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To answer the charge that Jesus welcomes sinners by revealing the seeking, rejoicing heart of God, to define repentance as coming home to a Father who runs to meet us, and to confront the self-righteous who resent grace with the open plea to the elder brother.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The parables describe God's character and the joy of heaven rather than issue commands, but they carry a searching application: come home like the younger son, and do not stand outside the celebration like the elder. The grumblers are meant to see themselves and choose.

debates.question: Who is the central figure of the third parable?

views.label: The father and his lavish, seeking love
views.case: The parable is bracketed by a father with two sons and turns on his running welcome and his plea to the elder; the title 'prodigal' fits the father's reckless generosity as much as the son's reckless waste. Both sons are foils to display the Father's heart, which is the answer to the grumble of verse 2.

views.label: The younger son and his repentance
views.case: The dramatic arc follows the younger son from demand to degradation to return, and the classic reading holds him up as the model of repentance coming home. His 'coming to his senses' shows what turning to God looks like.

debates.bearing: Whether we name it the father or the younger son, the point converges: repentance is coming home, and it is met by a love that runs; the Father's joy, not the sinner's worthiness, fills the scene.

debates.question: Why does the parable end with the elder brother unresolved?

views.label: A direct appeal to the grumbling Pharisees
views.case: The elder son is the mirror of the scribes and Pharisees of verse 2, dutiful yet joyless, resenting grace shown to sinners. Leaving him outside, with the father still pleading, presses the hearers to decide whether they will come in and rejoice.

views.label: A warning that self-righteousness also leaves one lost
views.case: The elder son is lost in his own way, near the father's house yet far from the father's heart, trusting his record rather than his father's love. The open ending warns that respectable religion can shut a person out of the feast as surely as wild living.

debates.bearing: Both readings turn the parable on the reader: grace exposes not only the runaway but the resentful, and the Father pleads with both to come in and share His joy.

study.originalLanguages: The keyword that rises through all three parables is chara, joy: the shepherd's joy, the woman's joy, the father's feast, and above all the joy of heaven and of God's angels over one sinner who returns. To be found is to metanoeo, to repent, to turn and come home, pictured as a sheep carried, a coin recovered, a son coming to his senses. The father, seeing his ruined boy still far off, is seized with splanchnizomai, a gut-deep compassion, and runs to him, the most vivid glimpse in Scripture of how God feels toward returning sinners. And the refrain apollymi, lost, binds the three: what was lost is found, what was dead is alive again. The vocabulary preaches the gospel: God seeks, God feels, God rejoices.

typology.type: God seeking His scattered sheep, Ezekiel 34:11-16
typology.fulfillment: The shepherd who leaves the ninety-nine and goes after the one lost sheep, Luke 15:4-7.

typology.type: The covering of shame, as God clothed Adam and Eve
typology.fulfillment: The father clothing his returned son in the best robe, ring, and sandals, Luke 15:22.
The celebration over the son who was dead and is alive again, anticipating the kingdom's table, Luke 15:23-24.

study.christ: Jesus is the shepherd who searches for the one lost sheep and carries it home on His shoulders, the very seeking love of God stepping into the pasture. He is the friend of tax collectors and sinners whose welcome scandalized the pious, and in that welcome we see the father running down the road with his robes hitched up, shaming himself to reach his son first. The costly grace of the fattened calf points beyond itself to the cross, where the price of the feast is paid. In Christ, God does not stand aloof from the perishing but goes out, finds, embraces, and rejoices.
study.god: The God of Luke 15 is a seeking, running, celebrating Father. He does not wait passively for the lost to earn their way back but lights the lamp, sweeps the house, scans the horizon, and sprints down the road when He sees his son. He is moved from the depths with compassion, quick to clothe and quick to feast, and His joy overflows to fill heaven itself. Yet He is also the Father who leaves the party to plead with the sullen elder son, honoring even the resentful with His entreaty. This is a God whose deepest response to the returning sinner is not reluctance but joy.

crossReferences.note: I Myself will search for My sheep and seek them out, the promise fulfilled in the seeking shepherd.
The Son of Man came to seek and to save the lost, the theme of all three parables in a single line.
While we were still sinners, Christ died for us, the costly love behind the father's welcome.
Christ Jesus came into the world to save sinners, the very people He welcomed and ate with.

study.oneStory: Answering the grumble that He welcomes sinners, Jesus opens a window onto the joy at the heart of God: a shepherd seeking, a woman searching, a father running to embrace the son who was dead and is alive again. On the road to Jerusalem He shows why He came, to seek and save the lost, and He leaves the elder brother standing at the door so that every hearer must decide whether to come in and share the Father's joy or stand outside it in the cold of self-righteousness.

quiz.q: Why does Jesus tell these three parables?

options.text: To answer the Pharisees and scribes who grumbled that He welcomes sinners and eats with them.
options.note: Luke 15:1-3 sets the parables as a direct reply to the grumbling.

options.text: To teach that God cares about lost and forgotten things.
options.note: True in substance, but the specific occasion is the objection to His welcoming sinners.

options.text: To encourage shepherds and housekeepers in their daily work.
options.note: The everyday images serve a deeper point about God's joy over repentant sinners.

options.text: To warn sinners to stay away from Him.
options.note: The opposite: He welcomes sinners, and heaven rejoices when they come.

quiz.q: What does heaven do when one sinner repents?

options.text: Heaven rejoices, and there is joy in the presence of God's angels.
options.note: Luke 15:7,10 declares joy in heaven over one sinner who repents.

options.text: God notes the sinner's return with approval.
options.note: True but understated; Scripture speaks not of quiet approval but of overflowing joy.

options.text: The angels record the repentance for the day of judgment.
options.note: The text speaks of present rejoicing, not merely record-keeping.

options.text: Heaven remains unmoved unless many repent at once.
options.note: The joy is expressly over one sinner who repents.

quiz.q: How does the father respond when the younger son returns?

options.text: While the son is still far off, the father is filled with compassion, runs to him, embraces him, and throws a feast.
options.note: Luke 15:20-24 shows the running, compassionate welcome and celebration.

options.text: The father forgives the son and restores him to the family.
options.note: True, but it misses the striking picture of the father running while the son is still far off.

options.text: The father waits for the son to finish his prepared apology first.
options.note: The father runs and embraces him before the son can finish; the welcome precedes the full speech.

options.text: The father makes the son work as a hired servant to earn his place.
options.note: The son is restored as a son with robe, ring, and feast, not demoted to a servant.

quiz.q: What is the point of the elder brother in the third parable?

options.text: He mirrors the grumbling religious leaders, dutiful yet joyless, resenting grace shown to sinners.
options.note: The elder son embodies the Pharisees of verse 2 who resent Jesus welcoming sinners.

options.text: He shows that self-righteousness can leave a person outside the feast.
options.note: True, but it does not name the pointed link to the grumbling leaders Jesus is addressing.

options.text: He teaches that hard work should be rewarded.
options.note: The parable exposes his resentment, not a lesson about rewarding diligence.

options.text: He proves the younger son should not have been forgiven.
options.note: The father defends the celebration; the elder son's complaint is gently corrected.

quiz.q: What does the repeated phrase 'lost and found' reveal about God?

options.text: God actively seeks the lost until He finds them and rejoices to restore them to life.
options.note: The shepherd, the woman, and the father all seek and rejoice, revealing the seeking God.

options.text: God is willing to receive those who find their own way back.
options.note: Partly true of the son, but the sheep and coin are sought and found by another, stressing God's initiative.

options.text: God keeps careful count of who is missing.
options.note: The counting serves the deeper truth of a God who searches and rejoices, not mere bookkeeping.

options.text: God leaves the lost to return entirely on their own.
options.note: The shepherd goes after the sheep and the father runs; God takes the initiative.

questions: The shepherd went after the one until he found it. Where do you need to know that God has been seeking you, not waiting for you to find your own way back?
The father ran while his son was still a long way off. Does your picture of God match this, a Father sprinting to meet returning sinners, or do you imagine Him reluctant and cold?
The younger son rehearsed a speech about becoming a servant, but the father restored him as a son. Are you trying to earn back a place that grace wants to give you freely?
The elder brother kept the rules yet missed the father's heart and stood outside the party. Where might dutiful religion be leaving you resentful of the grace God shows to others?
Heaven throws a party over one sinner who repents. Whose repentance could you celebrate this week, and does the joy of God shape how you treat people who come home?

translationNotes.note: The ten silver coins are Greek drachmas, each worth about a day's wages; the single lost coin may have been part of a woman's savings or dowry, which explains the diligent, lamp-lit search.

Some manuscripts add to the son's confession the line 'Make me like one of your hired servants' from his rehearsed speech in verse 19; the father cuts the speech short with restoration before the son can finish.

'Was dead and is alive again, was lost and is found' is the refrain of the whole chapter. Being lost is pictured as a kind of death, and being found as resurrection, language that reaches its fullness in the gospel of Christ.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for Luke 15:1-32. Headings, line breaks, footnote markers, and speaker labels were skipped when assembling verse text; no em or en dashes occur in this passage. Sermon status set to none; no sermon from the trusted cluster was verified this session.
